14th Street urban renewal area, 1st-3rd action years : environmental impact statement.

The 14th Street Urban Renewal Area project in Washington, D.C. aims to revitalize a 340-acre area that was heavily damaged during the 1968 civil disorders. The plan involves acquiring and redeveloping land, clearing blighted areas, and rehabilitating existing structures. The goal is to eliminate substandard conditions, provide housing for low and moderate-income families, and create new commercial and employment opportunities. The project is expected to improve the community's living conditions and stimulate economic growth, but it may also cause temporary disruptions and displacement of residents.
14th Street Urban Renewal Area, Washington, D.C.
Department of Housing and Urban Development
District of Columbia Redevelopment Land Agency

The 14th Street Urban Renewal Area project was proposed in response to the significant damage inflicted on the area during the civil unrest of 1968. It aimed to revitalize a blighted neighborhood by improving living conditions and providing opportunities for low and moderate-income families. While there was some public concern regarding potential displacement and disruptions, the project ultimately sought to foster economic growth and community improvement.
United States. Department of Housing and Urban Development

"The 14th Street Project Area was a major target of destruction during the 1968 civil disorders, sustaining approximately half of the estimated total property loss of over $13 million in the District." — Introduction – Location and Description of 14th Street Area

"The primary objective of the 14th Street Urban Renewal Plan is to eliminate substandard building conditions and other blighting conditions and to provide for the rebuilding of areas damaged during the civil disorders." — 14th Street Land Use Objectives
